questions.question: Explain the 8 primitive types in Java and their memory sizes.
keyPoints: byte (8-bit), short (16-bit), int (32-bit), long (64-bit) - integer types
float (32-bit), double (64-bit) - floating point types
char (16-bit) - character type
boolean - logical type (typically 1 bit logically, 1 byte in arrays)

questions.question: What is the difference between widening and narrowing type conversion? Give examples.
keyPoints: Widening: automatic conversion from smaller to larger type (int to long, float to double) - no data loss
Narrowing: explicit conversion from larger to smaller type (double to int, long to byte) - requires casting, may lose data
Example: int x = 100; long y = x; (widening)
int z = (int) 3.99; // results in 3 (narrowing)

questions.question: Why do byte and short not get promoted to byte or short in arithmetic expressions?
keyPoints: In Java, byte, short, and char are promoted to int during arithmetic operations
This is to prevent overflow issues during calculations
Result is always at least int, may need explicit casting back
Example: byte a=10, b=20; byte c = a+b; // error, needs cast

questions.question: What are the default values of primitive types when used as instance variables?
keyPoints: byte, short, int, long: 0
float, double: 0.0
char: '\u0000' (null character, value 0)
boolean: false

questions.question: Explain why Java has both float and double types.
keyPoints: float is 32-bit single precision (6-7 significant digits)
double is 64-bit double precision (15 significant digits)
double provides more precision but uses more memory
float may be used for memory efficiency when precision is not critical

questions.question: What is the output of: System.out.println('A' + 1); and System.out.println("A" + 1);
keyPoints: 'A' + 1 prints 66 because char promotes to int (A=65 ASCII)
"A" + 1 prints A1 because + is string concatenation
This demonstrates the difference between primitive char and String reference type

questions.question: Write a Java program to demonstrate type conversion between all primitive numeric types. Show widening, narrowing with casting, and automatic promotion in expressions.
keyPoints: Program declares variables of all numeric types
Shows widening conversion (automatic)
Shows narrowing conversion (requires cast)
Demonstrates expression promotion rules
Shows what happens with overflow/underflow
Output demonstrating each conversion type

questions.question: Explain the design decision of having primitive types in Java despite everything being an object. Discuss memory allocation, performance, and the need for wrapper classes.
keyPoints: Primitives stored in stack, objects in heap - stack is faster
No object overhead for primitives - less memory per value
Design choice for performance-critical applications
Wrapper classes (Integer, Double, etc.) needed for: Collections API, Generic types, Null values, Utility methods

questions.question: Explain the range calculation for byte and short primitive types. Why is byte range -128 to 127 and not -127 to 128?
keyPoints: 8-bit can represent 256 values (2^8)
Two's complement representation for signed integers
For n-bit signed: range is -2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1)-1
One value used for 0, remaining 255 split between positive and negative
This allows symmetric range with efficient computation
